content:
```json
{
  "idea": "Dynamic Semantic Sparse Attention for Efficient MLA Inference",
  "idea_description": "This research proposes dynamic, content-adaptive sparse attention for MLA language models, where attention patterns are selected per token and layer based on query semantics. The mechanism combines generalized multi-query attention, asynchronous sparse attention, and semantic-guided sparsity selection to reduce computation and memory while preserving answer fidelity, long-context retrieval reliability, and hardware-efficient scheduling under distribution shift. Unlike AsyncTLS, which uses fixed sparsity, this work dynamically adjusts attention patterns, enabling different sparse K/V groupings or zeroing based on local query semantics. It also reuses FlashAttention-style tiled kernel computation and GQA-style shared K/V heads to maintain speed, memory, and quality.",
  "technical_plan": {
    "target_problem": "Efficient generative LLM inference on MLA architectures under distribution shift, with fixed sparse attention sacrificing semantic adaptability.",
    "core_method": "Semantic-guided dynamic sparse attention that selects K/V heads, attention blocks, or token-level sparsity patterns per token and layer using query-aware semantic cues, combined with asynchronous computation and shared K/V heads.",
    "limitation_addressed": "Fixed inference-time sparsity patterns in AsyncTLS fail to adapt to changing query semantics while preserving answer fidelity, long-context retrieval, and hardware-efficient scheduling.",
    "expected_result": "A dynamic sparse attention mechanism that achieves AsyncTLS-style operator and end-to-end speedup while selecting different attention patterns per token and layer, with preserved answer quality and retrieval reliability on MLA-based models."
  },
  "lineage_connection": {
    "source_ideas": [
      "AsyncTLS: Efficient Generative LLM Inference with Asynchronous Two-level Sparse Attention",
      "GQA: Training Generalized Multi-Query Transformer Models from Multi-Head Checkpoints",
      "FlashAttention-2: Faster Attention with Better Parallelism and Work Partitioning"
    ],
    "reused_parts": [
      "Asynchronous two-level sparse attention from AsyncTLS",
      "Group query attention and shared K/V heads from GQA",
      "IO-aware tiled kernel computation and improved parallelism from FlashAttention-2"
    ],
    "changed_parts": [
      "Fixed inference-time sparsity is replaced by dynamic, content-adaptive per-token and per-layer sparsity selection based on query semantics.",
      "Sparse attention is extended beyond AsyncTLS block+token sparsity to include semantic-guided K/V grouping and zeroing.",
      "Semantic cues guide which attention patterns are activated, rather than relying on fixed MLA sparsity patterns."
    ],
    "why_next_step": "AsyncTLS achieves speedups but uses fixed MLA sparsity; GQA and FlashAttention provide memory and compute-efficient attention mechanisms that can be combined with semantic adaptation to enable dynamic sparse attention for efficient inference.",
    "lineage_risk": "The proposed semantic adaptation may be difficult to implement efficiently or may degrade answer fidelity if semantic cues are noisy, inaccurate, or not well aligned with attention relevance."
  },
  "evaluation_plan": {
    "setup": "Train and evaluate on large MLA-based language model inference benchmarks, including long-context retrieval and generation under distribution shift, measuring speed, throughput, memory use, answer fidelity, retrieval accuracy, and hardware-efficient scheduling.",
    "baselines": [
      "AsyncTLS: Asynchronous Two-level Sparse Attention on MLA",
      "MHA: Standard multi-head attention",
      "GQA: Group query attention with fixed sparse K/V sharing",
      "FlashAttention-2: Fast, parallel attention with tiled kernels"
    ],
    "metrics": [
      "Operator speedup",
      "End-to-end throughput",
      "KV-cache memory usage",
      "Answer fidelity on generation tasks",
prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Systems / Efficient LLM.
Below are 6 important recent papers in this area, presented in random order. Each item contains only the paper title and a brief abstract; no lineage order, structured source-change fields, or evolutionary dynamics labels are provided.

**Frontier Question**:
AsyncTLS achieves inference speedup via sparse attention on MLA but the sparsity pattern is fixed at inference time. What mechanism would enable dynamic, content-adaptive sparsity that selects different attention patterns per token and layer based on query semantics while preserving answer fidelity, long-context retrieval reliability, and hardware-efficient scheduling under distribution shift?

[Paper 1] GQA: Training Generalized Multi-Query Transformer Models from Multi-Head Checkpoints
Brief Abstract: GQA: group query attention — share one K/V head across G query heads; reduces KV-cache memory by G× while maintaining quality near MHA.

[Paper 2] FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness
Brief Abstract: FlashAttention: IO-aware tiled kernel computation avoids materializing the N×N attention matrix; 2-4x speedup, sublinear memory in sequence length.

[Paper 3] FlashAttention-2: Faster Attention with Better Parallelism and Work Partitioning
Brief Abstract: FlashAttention-2: better parallelism over sequence dimension and improved work partitioning across thread blocks; achieves ~70% A100 MFU.

[Paper 4] DeepSeek-V2: A Strong, Economical, and Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Language Model
Brief Abstract: MLA (Multi-head Latent Attention): compress KV cache via low-rank joint projection, 93.3% KV-cache reduction while outperforming MHA quality.

[Paper 5] Attention Is All You Need
Brief Abstract: Transformer: multi-head self-attention replacing RNNs; enables parallelizable training; defines the O(n²) KV-cache bottleneck that all subsequent work addresses.

[Paper 6] AsyncTLS: Efficient Generative LLM Inference with Asynchronous Two-level Sparse Attention
Brief Abstract: Asynchronous block+token sparse attention specifically optimized for MLA architectures; achieves 1.2×-10.0× operator speedup and 1.3×-4.7× end-to-end throughput improvement on MLA-based models by decoupling block-level and token-level sparsity computation with asynchronous memory transfer.

Based on the frontier question and the papers above, generate a new research idea.

Return ONLY valid JSON. Do not wrap it in Markdown fences.
{
  "idea": "<short name of the proposed idea, 5-15 words>",
  "idea_description": "<150-250 words describing the full research idea>",
  "technical_plan": {
    "target_problem": "<specific problem, dataset, population, or phenomenon>",
    "core_method": "<technical method or mechanism, not a vague goal>",
    "limitation_addressed": "<frontier limitation repaired by this idea>",
    "expected_result": "<scientific result or claim if successful>"
  },
  "lineage_connection": {
    "source_ideas": ["<paper title or source idea used by this proposal>", "..."],
    "reused_parts": ["<specific method, evidence, data, or problem framing reused from the sources>", "..."],
    "changed_parts": ["<specific modified, replaced, combined, or newly added part>", "..."],
    "why_next_step": "<why this idea follows from the provided context>",
    "lineage_risk": "<most likely way this source connection could be wrong>"
  },
  "evaluation_plan": {
    "setup": "<concrete experiment, benchmark, proof, or analysis setup>",
    "baselines": ["<baseline>", "..."],
    "metrics": ["<metric>", "..."],
    "risks": ["<risk>", "..."]
  }
}

metadata.system_prompt: You are a scientific lineage analyst and idea generator. Respond with a single JSON object inside ```json ... ``` fences matching the schema requested in the user prompt. No commentary outside the JSON.
